---
title: "Tumour Spheroid Irradiation with GSM²-ABM"
subtitle: "A walkthrough of the spheroid irradiation example from the GSM2_Julia package"
author: "Francesco G. Cordoni"
date: today
format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-title: "Contents"
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: true
    code-copy: true
    highlight-style: github
    embed-resources: true
    fig-align: center
    number-sections: true
execute:
  eval: true
  echo: true
  cache: true
---

## Overview {.unnumbered}

This document walks through the
[`spheroid.jl`](https://github.com/francescogcordoni/GSM2_Julia/blob/main/example/spheroid.jl)
example from the [GSM2_Julia](https://github.com/francescogcordoni/GSM2_Julia)
repository. The example simulates the irradiation of a three-dimensional tumour
spheroid with a charged-particle beam, followed by an agent-based model (ABM)
simulation of post-irradiation cell dynamics over a 72-hour window.

The pipeline combines two modelling components:

- **GSM²** (Generalized Stochastic Microdosimetric Model): a fully probabilistic
  framework for radiation-induced DNA damage induction, repair, and misrepair,
  resolved at the single-cell and subnuclear scale.
- **ABM** (Agent-Based Model): an event-driven continuous-time Markov simulation
  of cell-cycle progression, proliferation, migration, and death following
  irradiation.

Together, these two components provide a mechanistic, single-cell account of
tumour response to particle irradiation, from the physics of energy deposition
to the long-term evolution of the surviving cell population.

The full simulation pipeline is illustrated below.

```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
    A["🔧 1. Parameters\nparticle · energy · dose · geometry"]
    B["⚙️ 2. Setup\nGSM² · lattice · population · O₂"]
    C["☢️ 3. Monte Carlo dose deposition\nAmorphous Track model"]
    D["🧬 4. DNA damage sampling\nPoisson lesion induction"]
    E["📊 5. GSM² survival probability\nper-cell stochastic SSA"]
    F["⏱️ 6. Stochastic timers\nrepair · death · cycle"]
    G["🔬 7. ABM simulation\ncell dynamics over 72 h"]
    H["📈 8. Output\nplots · CSVs · snapshots"]

    A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G --> H

    style A fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#3b82f6
    style B fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#3b82f6
    style C fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#ef4444
    style D fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#ef4444
    style E fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#eab308
    style F fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#eab308
    style G fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#22c55e
    style H fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#22c55e
```

---

## Setup

Load packages and source utilities. This is the same pattern used by every other
script in the repository.

```{julia}
#| label: setup
#| output: false

# Switch to the global Julia environment where all simulation packages are installed
import Pkg
Pkg.activate(joinpath(homedir(), ".julia", "environments",
                      "v$(VERSION.major).$(VERSION.minor)"))

# Load packages in Main.Notebook
using Base.Threads, Distributed
using CSV, DataFrames, Distributions, Random
using Plots, StatsPlots, Statistics, StatsBase
using ProgressBars, ProgressMeter
using GLM, Optim, LsqFit
using JLD2, DelimitedFiles
using InlineStrings, SparseArrays, Printf, CUDA
using Statistics: mean

# Force GR headless mode before any plot is created
ENV["GKSwstype"] = "100"

# Load packages in Main as well (utility functions defined there need them)
@eval Main begin
    using Base.Threads, Distributed, CSV, DataFrames, Distributions, Random
    using Plots, StatsPlots, Statistics, StatsBase, ProgressBars, ProgressMeter
    using GLM, Optim, LsqFit, JLD2, DelimitedFiles
    using InlineStrings, SparseArrays, Printf, CUDA
    using Statistics: mean
end

# Include utilities into Main so that functions and @eval Main injections share scope
Base.include(Main, joinpath(@__DIR__, "..", "src", "load_utilities.jl"))

# Import all types from Main into Main.Notebook
using Main: GSM2, Ion, Irrad, AT, Cell, CellPopulation, SimulationTimeSeries, Voxel, Track

# Import all utility + plot functions from Main into Main.Notebook so they can
# be called without the Main. prefix, and all @eval Main globals remain consistent
import Main: setup_GSM2!, setup_IonIrrad!, setup_cell_lattice!, setup_cell_population!,
             setup_irrad_conditions!, set_oxygen!, calculate_beam_properties,
             load_stopping_power,
             print_phase_distribution, print_simulation_summary, survival_ci,
             MC_dose_CPU!, MC_loop_damage!,
             compute_cell_survival_GSM2!, compute_times_domain!, run_simulation_abm!,
             to_dataframe, deepcopy,
             plot_cell_cycle_distribution, plot_phase_proportions_alive,
             plot_scalar_cell, plot_damage, plot_times, plot_initial_distributions,
             plot_cell_dynamics, plot_phase_dynamics, plot_phase_proportions,
             plot_phase_stacked, plot_cycling_vs_quiescent, plot_growth_rate,
             plot_stem_dynamics, plot_simulation_results, plot_analysis_dashboard,
             plot_phase_proportions_timeseries, plot_phase_duration_distribution,
             plot_snapshot_comparison, plot_phase_comparison_before_after,
             plot_spatial_distribution, create_spatial_animation

# Load stopping power and make it available in Main (needed by setup_IonIrrad!)
sp = load_stopping_power()
@eval Main sp = $sp

gr()
println("Threads available: ", nthreads())
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## Running the full example from the command line

```bash
julia --threads auto example/spheroid.jl
```

Or from an interactive session:

```julia
include("example/spheroid.jl")
```

Make sure all packages listed in `spheroid.jl` are installed in your Julia environment
before running.
:::

---

## Irradiation parameters

All user-facing parameters are collected in one block. Changing the irradiation
condition requires editing only this block — no other part of the code needs to
be modified.

```{julia}
#| label: parameters
#| output: false

PARTICLE      = "12C"
ENERGY_MEV_U  = 80.0
DOSE_GY       = 1.0

TUMOR_RADIUS  = 350.0
R_CELL        = 15.0
X_BOX         = 550.0
X_VOXEL       = 700.0

RD            = 0.8
RN            = 7.2

TYPE_AT       = "KC"
TRACK_SEG     = false
TARGET_GEOM   = "circle"
CALC_TYPE     = "full"
TERMINAL_TIME = 72.0
SNAPSHOT_HOURS = [0, 6, 12, 24, 48, 72]
NAT_APO       = 1e-10

OUTDIR = joinpath(@__DIR__, "output", "spheroid")
mkpath(OUTDIR)
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## Switching particle species
Changing `PARTICLE` from `"1H"` to `"12C"` and adjusting `ENERGY_MEV_U` is all
that is needed. LET, fluence, and Amorphous Track parameters are computed
automatically from the stopping-power tables.
:::

---

## Phase-specific GSM² parameters

The GSM² model assigns three rate constants to each cell-cycle phase: the repair
rate $r$, the sublethal-to-lethal conversion rate $a$, and the binary misrepair
rate $b$. These are calibrated to reproduce the phase-specific LQ parameters
reported by Sinclair (1966).

```{julia}
#| label: gsm2-params
#| output: false

A_G1 = 0.01287;  B_G1 = 0.04030;  R_G1 = 2.7805
A_S  = 0.00589;  B_S  = 0.05794;  R_S  = 5.8401
A_G2 = 0.02431;  B_G2 = 5.705e-5; R_G2 = 1.7720
A_AVG= 0.01481;  B_AVG= 0.01266;  R_AVG= 2.5657
```

| Phase  | $\alpha$ (Gy⁻¹) | $\beta$ (Gy⁻²) | Radiosensitivity   |
|--------|-----------------|----------------|--------------------|
| G₁     | 0.351           | 0.040          | Moderate           |
| S      | 0.124           | 0.029          | Most resistant     |
| G₂/M   | 0.793           | 0.000          | Most sensitive     |

: Phase-specific LQ parameters used for GSM² calibration. {.striped .hover}

The purely linear response in G₂/M ($\beta = 0$) reflects the dominance of
single-track lethal events, reproduced in GSM² by a near-zero binary misrepair
rate $b$.

---

## Spheroid setup

The setup stage builds the three-dimensional cell lattice, assigns biological
attributes to each cell (cycle phase, oxygenation, damage vectors, neighbour
lists), and computes the beam geometry.

```{julia}
#| label: setup-simulation
#| output: true

gsm2_cycle    = Array{GSM2}(undef, 4)
gsm2_cycle[1] = GSM2(R_G1, A_G1, B_G1, RD, RN)
gsm2_cycle[2] = GSM2(R_S,  A_S,  B_S,  RD, RN)
gsm2_cycle[3] = GSM2(R_G2, A_G2, B_G2, RD, RN)
gsm2_cycle[4] = GSM2(R_AVG, A_AVG, B_AVG, RD, RN)

# setup_* functions are in Main; they inject globals into Main via @eval Main
setup_GSM2!(R_AVG, A_AVG, B_AVG, RD, RN)
setup_IonIrrad!(DOSE_GY, ENERGY_MEV_U, PARTICLE; type_AT=TYPE_AT)

N_sideVox   = Int(floor(2 * X_BOX / X_VOXEL))
N_CellsSide = 2 * convert(Int64, floor(X_BOX / (2 * R_CELL)))

setup_cell_lattice!(TARGET_GEOM, X_BOX, R_CELL, N_sideVox, N_CellsSide;
                    ParIrr="false", track_seg=TRACK_SEG, full_cycle=true)

# Inject spatial geometry into Main (setup_cell_population! reads these from Main)
@eval Main begin
    tumor_radius = $TUMOR_RADIUS
    X_voxel      = $X_VOXEL
    X_box        = $X_BOX
end

setup_cell_population!(TARGET_GEOM, X_BOX, R_CELL, N_sideVox, N_CellsSide,
                        gsm2_cycle[4]; ParIrr="false")

# Sync all globals injected into Main by setup functions into Main.Notebook scope
for _sym in [:gsm2, :center_x, :center_y, :domain,
              :type_AT, :dose, :A_, :A, :Z, :LET, :ion, :irrad,
              :Rc, :Rp, :Rk, :Kp, :DoseRate_h,
              :N, :nodes_positions, :cell_df, :track_seg, :full_cycle, :ParIrr,
              :rel_center_x, :rel_center_y, :df_center_x, :df_center_y, :at, :num_cols,
              :tumor_radius, :X_voxel, :X_box]
    isdefined(Main, _sym) && @eval $_sym = Main.$_sym
end

setup_irrad_conditions!(ion, irrad, TYPE_AT, cell_df, TRACK_SEG)
for _sym in [:irrad_cond, :irrad_cond_original_layers, :lets, :energies, :num_energy_steps]
    isdefined(Main, _sym) && @eval $_sym = Main.$_sym
end

set_oxygen!(cell_df; plot_oxygen=false)

N_init = count(cell_df.is_cell .== 1)
println("Spheroid: $N_init cells")
print_phase_distribution(cell_df; label="pre-irradiation")

R_beam, x_beam, y_beam = calculate_beam_properties(
    CALC_TYPE, TARGET_GEOM, X_BOX, X_VOXEL, TUMOR_RADIUS)

F    = irrad.dose / (1.602e-9 * LET)
Npar = round(Int, F * π * R_beam^2 * 1e-8)
Npar == 0 && error("Npar = 0: check beam radius and dose settings")
zF   = irrad.dose / Npar

println("Beam: R_beam = $(round(R_beam, digits=1)) µm  |  Npar = $Npar  |  zF = $(round(zF, digits=5)) Gy")

cell_df_pristine = deepcopy(cell_df)
```

### Initial cell-cycle distribution

A 3-D half-cut view of the spheroid coloured by cycle phase:

```{julia}
#| label: fig-cycle-init
#| fig-cap: "Initial 3-D cell-cycle distribution (half-cut view)."
#| fig-width: 10
#| fig-height: 6

display(plot_cell_cycle_distribution(cell_df; phase_plot=false, half_sphere=true))
```

Phase proportions as a bar chart:

```{julia}
#| label: fig-phase-init
#| fig-cap: "Phase proportion bar chart – pre-irradiation."
#| fig-width: 7
#| fig-height: 4

display(plot_phase_proportions_alive(cell_df;
    title_text="Phase distribution – pre-irradiation"))
```

### Oxygenation model

The oxygen distribution follows a quasi-static, radially symmetric profile with
three concentric zones:

::: {.callout-note}
## Oxygen zones
| Zone | Condition | O₂ level |
|------|-----------|----------|
| Normoxic outer shell | depth $\delta < \lambda$ | 7% (surface value) |
| Hypoxic viable rim | $\lambda \leq \delta < R - r_\mathrm{core}$ | Linear 7% → 0.1% |
| Necrotic/hypoxic core | $\delta \geq R - r_\mathrm{core}$ | 0.1% (floor) |

The penetration depth is $\lambda = 400\,\mu\mathrm{m}$.
:::

---

## Monte Carlo dose deposition

Dose deposition uses the Kiefer–Chatterjee parameterisation of the Amorphous
Track model. Each particle is propagated stochastically and the energy deposited
in each cell nucleus is computed from the radial dose distribution.

```{julia}
#| label: mc-dose
#| cache: true

println("--- Monte Carlo dose deposition ---")
@time MC_dose_CPU!(ion, Npar, R_beam, irrad_cond, cell_df,
                  df_center_x, df_center_y, at,
                  gsm2_cycle, TYPE_AT, TRACK_SEG)

println("--- Poisson DNA damage sampling ---")
@time MC_loop_damage!(ion, cell_df, irrad_cond, gsm2_cycle)
```

::: {.callout-note}
## Protons as worst case
High-energy protons require more particles to deliver the same dose as heavier
ions, making the Monte Carlo step slower for protons than for carbon at equal dose.
:::

### Dose distribution

Density of cell-absorbed doses with 3-D spatial map:

```{julia}
#| label: fig-dose
#| fig-cap: "Cell dose distribution: density (left) and 3-D spatial map (right)."
#| fig-width: 12
#| fig-height: 5

display(plot_scalar_cell(cell_df, :dose_cell))
```

Dose split by depth layer, revealing the Bragg-peak buildup:

```{julia}
#| label: fig-dose-layer
#| fig-cap: "Cell dose grouped by energy-step (depth) layer."
#| fig-width: 12
#| fig-height: 5

display(plot_scalar_cell(cell_df, :dose_cell; layer_plot=true))
```

---

## DNA damage sampling

Sublethal ($X$) and lethal ($Y$) lesions are sampled from Poisson distributions
whose rates are proportional to the local specific energy and modulated by the OER:

$$
Z_X^{(j,d)} \sim \mathrm{Poisson}\!\left(\kappa(\mathrm{LET}, [\mathrm{O_2}])\, z^{(j,d)}\right)
\qquad
Z_Y^{(j,d)} \sim \mathrm{Poisson}\!\left(\lambda(\mathrm{LET}, [\mathrm{O_2}])\, z^{(j,d)}\right)
$$

Total sublethal (X) lesions per cell:

```{julia}
#| label: fig-damage
#| fig-cap: "X-damage density across all alive cells."
#| fig-width: 10
#| fig-height: 5

display(plot_damage(cell_df))
```

X-damage split by depth layer:

```{julia}
#| label: fig-damage-layer
#| fig-cap: "X-damage density grouped by energy-step layer."
#| fig-width: 12
#| fig-height: 5

display(plot_damage(cell_df; layer_plot=true))
```

X-damage density + 3-D spatial map:

```{julia}
#| label: fig-dam-X
#| fig-cap: "Total sublethal (X) lesions per cell: density and 3-D spatial map."
#| fig-width: 12
#| fig-height: 5

display(plot_scalar_cell(cell_df, :dam_X_total))
```

Lethal (Y) lesions — any cell with `dam_Y_total > 0` has zero survival
probability in GSM²:

```{julia}
#| label: fig-dam-Y
#| fig-cap: "Total lethal (Y) lesions per cell: density and 3-D spatial map."
#| fig-width: 12
#| fig-height: 5

display(plot_scalar_cell(cell_df, :dam_Y_total))
```

X-damage vs absorbed dose scatter:

```{julia}
#| label: fig-dam-vs-dose
#| fig-cap: "X-damage vs cell dose scatter (one point per alive cell)."
#| fig-width: 7
#| fig-height: 5

let df_a = cell_df[cell_df.is_cell .== 1, :]
    p = scatter(df_a.dose_cell, df_a.dam_X_total;
                xlabel="Cell dose (Gy)", ylabel="Total X damage (DSBs)",
                title="X damage vs dose per cell",
                markersize=3, markerstrokewidth=0, alpha=0.5,
                label="", color=:steelblue)
    display(p)
end
```

---

## GSM² survival probability

The GSM² SSA evolves each cell's lesion state through repair, conversion, and
pairwise interaction events until survival (all sublethals repaired) or death
(first lethal lesion forms).

```{julia}
#| label: gsm2-survival
#| cache: true

println("--- GSM2 survival probability ---")
@time compute_cell_survival_GSM2!(cell_df, gsm2_cycle)

alive_mask = cell_df.is_cell .== 1
SF_mean    = mean(cell_df.sp[alive_mask])
p_hat, ci_lo, ci_hi = survival_ci(cell_df.sp[alive_mask])
println("Mean SP : $(round(SF_mean, digits=4))  " *
        "(Wilson 95% CI: $(round(ci_lo, digits=4)) – $(round(ci_hi, digits=4)))")

cell_df_irradiated = deepcopy(cell_df)
```

Survival probability density + 3-D spatial map (log₁₀ x-axis):

```{julia}
#| label: fig-sp
#| fig-cap: "Per-cell survival probability: density (log₁₀) and 3-D spatial map."
#| fig-width: 12
#| fig-height: 5

display(plot_scalar_cell(cell_df, :sp; xscale=:log10))
```

Phase proportions before and after irradiation:

```{julia}
#| label: fig-phase-compare
#| fig-cap: "Phase distribution: pre-irradiation (left) vs post-irradiation (right)."
#| fig-width: 12
#| fig-height: 5

p_phase_irrad = plot_phase_proportions_alive(cell_df_irradiated;
                    title_text="Phase distribution – post-irradiation")
display(plot(plot_phase_proportions_alive(cell_df_pristine;
         title_text="Phase distribution – pre-irradiation"),
     p_phase_irrad; layout=(1, 2), size=(1000, 400)))
```

---

## Stochastic repair and death timers

Each cell is assigned stochastic waiting times for repair completion, lethal
lesion formation, and the next cell-cycle event. These compete in the ABM's
global event queue.

```{julia}
#| label: compute-timers
#| cache: true

println("--- Stochastic repair and death timers ---")
@time compute_times_domain!(cell_df, gsm2_cycle;
                                  nat_apo       = NAT_APO,
                                  terminal_time = TERMINAL_TIME,
                                  verbose       = false,
                                  summary       = true)
```

Timer distributions with means annotated:

```{julia}
#| label: fig-times
#| fig-cap: "Stochastic timer distributions: death, recovery, and cell-cycle event times."
#| fig-width: 12
#| fig-height: 6

display(plot_times(cell_df; show_means=true, summary=true))
```

---

## ABM simulation

The ABM evolves the cell population over `TERMINAL_TIME` hours using a next-event
algorithm. Only affected cells are rescheduled at each step.

```{julia}
#| label: run-abm
#| cache: true

println("--- Agent-based model ($TERMINAL_TIME h) ---")
ts, snaps = @time run_simulation_abm!(cell_df;
                nat_apo           = NAT_APO,
                terminal_time     = TERMINAL_TIME,
                snapshot_times    = SNAPSHOT_HOURS,
                print_interval    = 6.0,
                verbose           = true,
                return_dataframes = false,
                update_input      = true)

N_final = count(cell_df.is_cell .== 1)
SF_abm  = N_final / N_init
println("\nFinal alive: $N_final / $N_init  (ABM SF = $(round(SF_abm, digits=4)))")
print_simulation_summary(ts)

snap_df = Dict(t => to_dataframe(snaps[t]; alive_only=true)
               for t in SNAPSHOT_HOURS if haskey(snaps, t))
```

| Category | Event |
|----------|-------|
| Repair / death | sublethal damage repair or lethal lesion formation (GSM²) |
| Cell cycle | G₁ → S → G₂ → M → division, or G₀ quiescence |
| Particle arrival | new energy deposition during protracted irradiation |
| Migration | random hop to an empty neighbouring lattice site |

: Event categories in the ABM global priority queue. {.striped .hover}

---

## Output

### Population dynamics

Total cell count vs time:

```{julia}
#| label: fig-dyn
#| fig-cap: "Total cell count over the 72-hour post-irradiation window."
#| fig-width: 10
#| fig-height: 5

display(plot_cell_dynamics(ts))
```

Per-phase cell counts vs time:

```{julia}
#| label: fig-phases-dyn
#| fig-cap: "G₁, S, G₂, M, and G₀ cell counts over time."
#| fig-width: 10
#| fig-height: 5

display(plot_phase_dynamics(ts))
```

Phase percentages vs time:

```{julia}
#| label: fig-prop
#| fig-cap: "Phase percentages of the surviving population over time."
#| fig-width: 10
#| fig-height: 5

display(plot_phase_proportions(ts))
```

Stacked area chart:

```{julia}
#| label: fig-stacked
#| fig-cap: "Stacked phase counts over time."
#| fig-width: 10
#| fig-height: 5

display(plot_phase_stacked(ts))
```

Cycling (G₁+S+G₂+M) vs quiescent (G₀):

```{julia}
#| label: fig-cyq
#| fig-cap: "Cycling vs quiescent cell counts over time."
#| fig-width: 10
#| fig-height: 5

display(plot_cycling_vs_quiescent(ts))
```

Sliding-window population growth rate:

```{julia}
#| label: fig-growth
#| fig-cap: "Sliding-window estimate of the population growth rate."
#| fig-width: 10
#| fig-height: 5

display(plot_growth_rate(ts; window_size=10))
```

Three-panel simulation summary:

```{julia}
#| label: fig-summary
#| fig-cap: "Three-panel simulation summary."
#| fig-width: 14
#| fig-height: 6

display(plot_simulation_results(ts))
```

Five-panel analysis dashboard:

```{julia}
#| label: fig-dash
#| fig-cap: "Five-panel analysis dashboard."
#| fig-width: 16
#| fig-height: 10

display(plot_analysis_dashboard(ts))
```

Phase proportions timeseries:

```{julia}
#| label: fig-prop-ts
#| fig-cap: "Phase proportions timeseries."
#| fig-width: 10
#| fig-height: 5

display(plot_phase_proportions_timeseries(ts;
    title_text="$PARTICLE $(ENERGY_MEV_U) MeV/u, $(DOSE_GY) Gy — Phase Dynamics"))
```

Phase duration proxy:

```{julia}
#| label: fig-dur
#| fig-cap: "Phase duration proxy derived from ABM event counts."
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: 5

display(plot_phase_duration_distribution(ts))
```

### Spatial snapshots

Phase bar chart at each snapshot time:

```{julia}
#| label: fig-snap-phase
#| fig-cap: "Phase distribution at each snapshot (0, 6, 12, 24, 48, 72 h)."
#| fig-width: 14
#| fig-height: 5

display(plot_snapshot_comparison(snap_df; metric=:cell_cycle))
```

Division capacity at each snapshot:

```{julia}
#| label: fig-snap-div
#| fig-cap: "Fraction of cells able to divide at each snapshot time."
#| fig-width: 14
#| fig-height: 5

display(plot_snapshot_comparison(snap_df; metric=:can_divide))
```

Phase comparison: pre-irradiation vs immediately post-irradiation:

```{julia}
#| label: fig-before-after
#| fig-cap: "Phase distribution before vs immediately after irradiation."
#| fig-width: 12
#| fig-height: 5

display(plot_phase_comparison_before_after(cell_df_pristine, cell_df_irradiated))
```

Phase comparison: pre-irradiation vs end of ABM:

```{julia}
#| label: fig-before-final
#| fig-cap: "Phase distribution before irradiation vs end of the 72-hour ABM."
#| fig-width: 12
#| fig-height: 5

display(plot_phase_comparison_before_after(cell_df_pristine, cell_df))
```

Spatial distribution at t = 0 and the final snapshot:

```{julia}
#| label: fig-spatial
#| fig-cap: "3-D cell-cycle spatial distribution: pre-irradiation (left) vs final snapshot (right)."
#| fig-width: 14
#| fig-height: 6

t_last     = maximum(keys(snap_df))
p_sp_init  = plot_spatial_distribution(cell_df_pristine;
                 color_by=:cell_cycle, title_text="Spatial – pre-irradiation")
p_sp_final = plot_spatial_distribution(snap_df[t_last];
                 color_by=:cell_cycle, title_text="Spatial – t=$(t_last)h")
display(plot(p_sp_init, p_sp_final; layout=(1, 2), size=(1200, 500)))
```

Animated GIF saved to disk:

```{julia}
#| label: anim
#| output: false

anim_file = joinpath(OUTDIR, "27_spatial_animation.gif")
create_spatial_animation(snap_df; output_file=anim_file, fps=2, color_by=:cell_cycle)
println("Animation saved to: $anim_file")
```

### Summary table

```{julia}
#| label: summary-table

summary_df = DataFrame(
    particle          = [PARTICLE],
    energy_MeV_u      = [ENERGY_MEV_U],
    dose_Gy           = [DOSE_GY],
    tumor_radius_um   = [TUMOR_RADIUS],
    N_cells_init      = [N_init],
    N_cells_final     = [N_final],
    Npar              = [Npar],
    SF_GSM2_mean      = [round(SF_mean,  digits=5)],
    SF_GSM2_CI_lo     = [round(ci_lo,    digits=5)],
    SF_GSM2_CI_hi     = [round(ci_hi,    digits=5)],
    SF_ABM            = [round(SF_abm,   digits=5)],
    ABM_hours         = [TERMINAL_TIME],
)
summary_df
```

---

## Further reading

- Cordoni et al. (2021). *Generalized stochastic microdosimetric model: the main
  formulation.* Physical Review E, 103, 012412.
- Cordoni et al. (2022). *Cell survival computation via the GSM²; part I: the
  theoretical framework.* Radiation Research, 197, 218–232.
- Missiaggia et al. (2024). *Cell survival computation via the GSM²; part II:
  numerical results.* Radiation Research.
- Battestini et al. (2026). *Mechanistic driven TCP and NTCP modeling for
  particle therapy.* (submitted)
- Sinclair, W.K. (1966). *Cyclic X-ray responses in mammalian cells in vitro.*
  Radiation Research, 33, 620–643.
- Hall & Giaccia. *Radiobiology for the Radiologist.* 8th edition.

---

## Session info {.appendix}

```{julia}
#| label: session-info
using Pkg
Pkg.status()
```

```{julia}
#| label: julia-version
VERSION
```
